examples: She blacked out from exhaustion after the marathon.
The heat caused several spectators to black out.
He blacked out briefly after the accident.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become conscious again after being unconscious.
examples: She came round a few minutes after fainting.
The patient came round slowly after the surgery.
When he finally came round, he couldn't remember anything.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To die (used to avoid saying 'die' when you think this might upset someone).
examples: His grandmother passed away peacefully in her sleep.
The famous author passed on after a long illness.
We were devastated when our beloved pet passed away.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To manage to stay alive after you have been very ill; to succeed in a very difficult situation.
examples: The doctors weren't sure she would pull through.
Despite the serious injuries, he managed to pull through.
The company pulled through the financial crisis.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become smaller and thinner than usual and not look fresh and healthy.
examples: The plants shrivelled up in the drought.
Without water, the leaves will shrivel up and die.
The fruit had shrivelled up in the sun.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To gradually become thinner and weaker over a period of time, usually because of an illness.
examples: The illness caused him to waste away over several months.
She watched her father waste away from the disease.
Without proper nutrition, patients can waste away quickly.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make someone gradually lose their energy or confidence.
examples: The constant criticism wore her down emotionally.
Years of stress had worn him down completely.
The long hours were wearing down even the most resilient workers.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ due to the extreme heat during the outdoor event.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Black out' means to suddenly lose consciousness.

fillInExercises.sentence: After a few minutes, the patient ___ round and opened his eyes.
Sadly, the elderly man ___ in his sleep last night.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pass away' is a euphemism for dying.

fillInExercises.sentence: Despite the critical condition, she managed to ___ through.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pull through' means to survive a serious illness.

fillInExercises.sentence: The flowers ___ because they weren't watered regularly.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Shrivel up' means to become dry and withered.

fillInExercises.sentence: The long illness caused him to ___ away to skin and bones.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Waste away' means to become thin and weak over time.

fillInExercises.sentence: Years of overwork had ___ her both physically and mentally.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Wear down' means to gradually exhaust someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: He ___ out during the intense workout session.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Black out' means to suddenly become unconscious.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: I heard that Tom [blacked out] during the basketball game.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Yes, but thankfully he [came round] quickly after that.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: The doctors were worried he might not [pull through] at first.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: The stress of training had really [worn him down] over time.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: His grandmother [passed away] last month too—it's been hard on him.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: No wonder he looked like he was [wasting away].

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: My tomato plants have completely [shrivelled up] in this heat.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: The vegetables will [waste away] if we don't water them more.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: This heat wave is really [wearing me down] physically.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Be careful not to [black out] while working outside.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Don't worry. If I do, I'll [come round] once I get some shade.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: We just need to [pull through] until the weather breaks.
